summary: DHH argues that digital communication apps connecting parents and schools have made the relationship worse, not better. Using Denmark's centralized school app Aula as his primary example, he describes how these tools create an endless stream of needless updates and parent threads that nobody actually wants. Despite his career building collaboration tools, he contends that some relationships benefit from friction and limited communication. He calls for eliminating these apps entirely, noting that both parents and teachers resent the constant connectivity they impose.
key_points: The limited school-home communication of the 80s and 90s (flyers, grade books, annual parent-teacher chats) worked well and didn't need digital replacement
School communication apps create obligation to monitor constant low-value updates just to catch the rare important message
Teachers suffer from the reduced friction too, getting inundated with direct messages from parents at a frequency and tone they'd never face in person
Not all connections benefit from removing friction — some barriers to interaction serve a valuable purpose
Both parents and teachers dislike these systems, yet everyone feels obligated to participate
money_quotes: Not all connections benefit from having the friction removed by digital systems. Some barriers to interaction are good, actually.
The fundamental premise that everything will be wonderful if we could just all be linked together everywhere, all the time is false.
I simply don't need the play-by-play from a teacher about what happened in class this week, every week. We talk to our kids at home.
I say that as someone who've spent a long career in the service of collaboration and communication tools. This is not the luddite perspective.
You're essentially obliged to partake in these non-stop chain mails, lest you miss a truly important or urgent message.

Parents and politicians are all, with some justification, so concerned about the dangers of screens these days. So let's start in our own interactions by burning the most useless use cases right away.
key_insight: Some human relationships function better with communication friction intact, and the reflexive digitization of every interaction often creates obligation and noise rather than genuine value.
